2022 · CoRL · Agents

Do As I Can, Not As I Say: Grounding Language in Robotic Affordances

A language model knows what to do; a robot knows what's possible. SayCan multiplies the two — the LLM's task knowledge (“Say”) times a learned affordance value function (“Can”) — to turn an open-ended kitchen request into a feasible, long-horizon sequence of skills a real robot can actually execute.

Michael Ahn, Anthony Brohan, Noah Brown, Yevgen Chebotar, Omar Cortes, Byron David, Chelsea Finn, Chuyuan Fu, Keerthana Gopalakrishnan, Karol Hausman, Alex Herzog, Daniel Ho, Jasmine Hsu, Julian Ibarz, Brian Ichter, Alex Irpan, Eric Jang, Rosario Jauregui Ruano, Kyle Jeffrey, Sally Jesmonth, Nikhil J. Joshi, Ryan Julian, Dmitry Kalashnikov, Yuheng Kuang, Kuang-Huei Lee, Sergey Levine, Yao Lu, Linda Luu, Carolina Parada, Peter Pastor, Jornell Quiambao, Kanishka Rao, Jarek Rettinghouse, Diego Reyes, Pierre Sermanet, Nicolas Sievers, Clayton Tan, Alexander Toshev, Vincent Vanhoucke, Fei Xia, Ted Xiao, Peng Xu, Sichun Xu, Mengyuan Yan, Andy Zeng · Robotics at Google & Everyday Robots
venue CoRL 2022 citations 3,369 topic Agents source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Large language models absorb an enormous amount of procedural common sense — ask one how to clean a spill and it produces a perfectly reasonable narrative. But that narrative is ungrounded: the model has never interacted with a physical environment, never seen the consequences of its words, and has no idea what the robot in front of it can actually do. Tell an LLM “I spilled my drink, can you help?” and it may suggest “try using a vacuum cleaner” — useless if there is no vacuum in the kitchen and the robot has never learned to operate one.

The gap SayCan targets is precisely this mismatch between a language model's semantic knowledge and an agent's physical capabilities. Prior approaches either trained policies end-to-end from instructions (no high-level common sense, and they fail to generalize to abstract or long-horizon commands), or prompted an LLM to produce a plan with no mechanism to keep that plan inside the robot's repertoire or appropriate to the current scene. A purely generative planner will happily emit steps the robot cannot perform, or that make no sense given what is in view — exactly the “Generative” baseline SayCan later ablates against.

SayCan's bet: keep the LLM as the source of what's useful for the goal, but constrain and re-weight its proposals by what's feasible right now, using affordances the robot has learned through reinforcement learning. The model says what it can do, not just what it can say.

// how it works

The method

SayCan assumes a fixed library of low-level skills Π. Each skill π (e.g. “pick up the sponge”) comes with a short language description ℓπ and an affordance function p(cπ | s, ℓπ) — the probability the skill succeeds if attempted from the current state s. The key observation is that in the sparse-reward RL setting (reward 1 on success, 0 otherwise), the skill's value function is exactly this affordance: it estimates the chance of completing the skill from here. That gives a clean, learned answer to “if I ask the robot to do this, can it?”

Separately, the LLM provides p(ℓπ | i) — the probability that a skill's description is a useful next step toward the user's instruction i. SayCan obtains this not by free generation but by scoring: it asks the LLM to rank every candidate skill description as a continuation of a fixed dialog prompt. The two probabilities are then simply multiplied, and the most probable skill is selected:

π★ = arg maxπ∈Π  p(cπ | s, ℓπ) · p(ℓπ | i) // Can (world-grounding, the value function) × Say (task-grounding, the LLM score)

This factorization falls out of a clean assumption: a skill that succeeds makes progress on the instruction with probability p(ℓπ | i), and a skill that fails makes no progress — so the probability that a skill both fits the goal and will work is the product. The selected skill is executed, its description is appended to the prompt, and the whole loop runs again until the LLM scores a termination token (“done”) highest. The plan is therefore emitted one feasible step at a time, and is fully interpretable: you can read off both halves of every decision.

The decision rule is a one-line algorithm: for every skill, evaluate the LLM score and the affordance, multiply, take the arg-max, execute, and repeat. Nothing is fine-tuned end-to-end — the LLM is frozen and used zero-shot, and the value functions are trained independently per skill.

Where the pieces come from

The planning LLM is 540B-parameter PaLM (the system is called PaLM-SayCan). The low-level skills are language-conditioned policies and value functions: behavior-cloning policies built on BC-Z, and RL value functions trained with MT-Opt in the Everyday Robots simulator with RetinaGAN sim-to-real transfer. Skills are specified by passing their text descriptions through a frozen Universal Sentence Encoder, so the planning LLM and the policy-conditioning LLM can differ — one reasons over many skills, the other expresses each skill granularly. The full repertoire is 551 skills spanning 7 skill families and 17 objects (picking, placing, rearranging, opening/closing drawers, and navigation).

// what they found

The results

The system is evaluated on an Everyday Robots mobile manipulator (7-DoF arm, two-fingered gripper) across 101 instructions from 7 instruction families in two office-kitchen environments, using 15 objects and 5 named locations. Two metrics: plan success rate (do 2 of 3 human raters agree the chosen skills could achieve the instruction?) and execution success rate (did the robot actually do it?).

In the mock kitchen where the skills were trained, PaLM-SayCan reached 84% planning and 74% execution. Moved to a real office kitchen, planning fell only 3 points (to 81%) but execution dropped 14 points (to 60%) — the plans transfer; the low-level skills are where the real world bites. Failures split roughly 65% LLM errors / 35% affordance errors, and the system was worst on the longest-horizon tasks, usually because the LLM terminated early (bringing one object but not the second).

The ablations are the load-bearing result, because they isolate why grounding matters (mock-kitchen numbers, totals across 101 tasks):

SystemWhat it removesPlanExecute
PaLM-SayCan— (full system)84%74%
No VFaffordance grounding (LLM only)67%—
Generativescoring → free generation + USE projection74%—
BC NLthe LLM entirely (policy on raw instruction)—0%
BC USEthe LLM (policy + USE skill projection)—9%

Drop the value function and planning falls from 84% to 67%; replace scoring with generation and it falls to 74%. Drop the LLM and the language-conditioned policies essentially collapse — BC NL executes 0% of tasks, and BC USE manages 9% (60% on single primitives, 0% on everything multi-step). Both halves of “Say × Can” are necessary.

Two further findings. Better LLM ⇒ better robot: swapping PaLM for the 137B FLAN model on the full robot loop drops planning from 84% to 70% and execution from 74% to 61% — PaLM roughly halves the planning error, the paper's claimed first demonstration that an improvement in the language model translates into a similar improvement in robotics. And chain-of-thought prompting extends SayCan: vanilla SayCan inherits the LLM's trouble with negation (“bring me a snack that isn't an apple”), but adding an “Explanation” step lets it reason through negations and constraints. Multilingual queries (Chinese, French, Spanish) showed almost no drop in planning success despite no multilingual design.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

SayCan is a genuinely influential idea, and the ablations are the right experiments — they cleanly show that removing either grounding signal hurts. But the evaluation is far softer than the 3,000-plus citations might suggest, and the headline numbers need careful reading.

First, this is an in-house benchmark with no external comparability. The 101 instructions across 7 families were designed by the authors (“inspired by” ALFRED and BEHAVIOR, but not those benchmarks), and every result lives in one robot, one object set, two near-identical office kitchens. There is no standardized embodied benchmark run, no comparison to a published baseline outside the paper's own ablations, and no other embodiment. The much-quoted “generalizes to the real kitchen” rests on a small distribution shift — same skills, same objects — and even there execution falls 14 points.

Second, success is human-rated, lenient, and circular. Both the metric and the RL reward come from the same 2-of-3 human-rater loop, so the thing being optimized and the thing being scored are defined by the same subjective judgment. “Plan success” only asks whether the chosen skills could achieve the goal, explicitly allows “multiple valid solutions,” and says nothing about efficiency or whether the affordance estimates are calibrated. The 84% headline is a planning number; the 74% execution number is gated by the low-level policies, which the authors themselves name as the primary bottleneck.

Third, the marquee “better LLM ⇒ better robot” claim is built on essentially two data points (PaLM 540B vs FLAN 137B) on a single in-house suite, confounding model size with training data and objective. The 8B/62B/540B scaling ablation is run on generative QA, not the full robot loop — so the clean “scaling the LM scales the robot” story is more suggestive than demonstrated. And there are no confidence intervals or seeds: with 11–15 instructions per family, a single flipped task moves a family score by 6–9 points, yet differences that small are read as signal.

baselines

Only internal ablations (No VF, Generative, BC NL, BC USE) — fair for isolating components, but no external or published baseline, and no standardized embodied benchmark (ALFRED/BEHAVIOR are cited as inspiration, not run).

contamination

No public test set to leak, but performance partly reflects a hand-curated skill vocabulary and an in-context prompt with worked examples — prompt engineering, not purely emergent grounding.

what's missed

Plan success (84%) is lenient and human-judged; execution (74%) is gated by skill quality; no calibration analysis of the value functions; single embodiment, single environment type, no CIs/seeds.

would raise confidence

Run an actual standardized benchmark; multiple environments/embodiments; report seeds and intervals; separate model-size from training-data in the LM ablation; and close the loop — SayCan only reads affordances at the current step and can't recover when a high-value skill fails (the gap Inner Monologue later fills).

// bottom line

The core mechanism — multiply the LLM's task score by a learned affordance to keep plans feasible — is simple, interpretable, and clearly does work; the ablations earn that conclusion. What the paper does not establish, despite how it's often cited, is a rigorous, externally-comparable measurement: the numbers are a self-designed, human-rated, single-setup demonstration, and the “scaling the LM scales the robot” headline is a two-point trend. A landmark recipe; a proof-of-concept evaluation.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Say × Can. Ground an LLM planner by multiplying its skill score p(ℓπ|i) by a learned affordance value function p(cπ|s,ℓπ) — the product is feasible and useful, and the LLM stays frozen and zero-shot.
  2. The value function is the affordance. In sparse-reward RL, a skill's value function already estimates “can I succeed from here?” — SayCan reuses it as the world-grounding signal, no separate affordance model needed.
  3. Both halves are load-bearing. Remove the value function and planning drops 84%→67%; remove the LLM and language-conditioned policies execute 0–9%. Neither the model's knowledge nor the robot's skills suffice alone.
  4. The LLM is the lever — but on a soft ruler. PaLM beats FLAN on the real robot (74% vs 61% execution), the headline link between LM quality and robot performance; just note it's a small, in-house, human-rated benchmark, so treat the trend as directional, not measured.
